coredata.dc:title: Stability ascent in perovskite solar cells employing star poly(3-hexylthiophene)/quantum dot nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Caixia
Agbolaghi, Samira
coredata.dc:description: Half-star poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT)/carbon quantum dot (CQD) and star P3HT/graphene quantum dot (GQD) supramolecules were constructed from crystallization of P3HT chains onto CQD and GQD nanoparticles, respectively. These nano-hybrids not only promoted the morphological parameters in upon-constructed state (594 and 612 nm), but also retained them during 150 day air aging as far as feasible (415 and 523 nm). The pure QDs were not capable of inducing crystallinity and improving morphology in CH3NH3PbI3 layers, instead, they froze perovskite precursors and decreased rate of degradation during aging. Forwardly, star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules simultaneously promoted efficacy and stability of perovskite solar cells. The best short circuit current density (Jsc = 24.71 and 21.94 mA/cm2), fill factor (FF = 77 and 75%), open circuit voltage (Voc = 0.96 and 0.94 V) and power conversion efficiency (PCE = 18.27 and 15.47%) were acquired for photovoltaics based on star and half-star nanostructures. The pre-designed supramolecules could perfectly interact with CH3NH3PbI3 constituents and decelerate the degradation of crystalline perovskite areas during long-term air aging procedures by freezing morphology of active layers. PCE of perovskite + star P3HT/GQD and perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD devices only decreased from 18.27 to 9.57% as well as from 15.47 to 5.84%, respectively, within 150 days air aging. In this aging duration, the performance of unmodified CH3NH3PbI3 photovoltaics was plummeted from 10.77 to zero%. Star and half-star nano-hybrids also made a prominent progress in charge transportation even during long-term air agings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: P3HT
Supramolecule
Quantum dot
Perovskite solar cell
Stability

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and SAED patterns (inset) of CQD nanoparticles (a); GQD nanoparticles (b); half-star P3HT/CQD (c); star P3HT/GQD (d).

XRD patterns (a), PL spectra (b), and UV–Vis spectra (c) of pure perovskite thin films and those modified with the GQD, star P3HT/GQD, CQD and half-star P3HT/CQD constituents in pristine state (filled curves) and after 150 days air aging (dashed curves).
FESEM images of perovskite (a), perovskite + CQD (b), perovskite + GQD (c), perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD (d) and perovskite + star P3HT/GQD (e).
FESEM images of perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD (a) and perovskite + star P3HT/GQD (b) films after 150 days air aging.
Configuration of a typical photovoltaic cell and the nanostructures separately applied in the active layers within time elapsing.

J-V curves of perovskite photovoltaics in pure and mixed states with the GQD, star P3HT/GQD, CQD and half-star P3HT/CQD precursors in pristine state (filled curves) and after 150 days air aging (dotted curves).

EIS spectra of pristine perovskite, perovskite + GQD, perovskite + star P3HT/GQD, perovskite + CQD and perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD solar cells in pristine state (filled curves) and after 150 days air aging (dashed curves).
The graphs of Rs (a) and Rtr (b) versus aging time.

EQE spectra of perovskite photovoltaics in pure and mixed states with the GQD, star P3HT/GQD, CQD and half-star P3HT/CQD precursors in pristine state (filled curves) and after 150 days air aging (dashed curves).
Photovoltaic characteristics and resistances of different perovskite solar cells.

head.ce:title: Stability ascent in perovskite solar cells employing star poly(3-hexylthiophene)/quantum dot nanostructures

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Nonferrous Metals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Half-star poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT)/carbon quantum dot (CQD) and star P3HT/graphene quantum dot (GQD) supramolecules were constructed from crystallization of P3HT chains onto CQD and GQD nanoparticles, respectively. These nano-hybrids not only promoted the morphological parameters in upon-constructed state (594 and 612 nm), but also retained them during 150 day air aging as far as feasible (415 and 523 nm). The pure QDs were not capable of inducing crystallinity and improving morphology in CH3NH3PbI3 layers, instead, they froze perovskite precursors and decreased rate of degradation during aging. Forwardly, star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules simultaneously promoted efficacy and stability of perovskite solar cells. The best short circuit current density (Jsc = 24.71 and 21.94 mA/cm2), fill factor (FF = 77 and 75%), open circuit voltage (Voc = 0.96 and 0.94 V) and power conversion efficiency (PCE = 18.27 and 15.47%) were acquired for photovoltaics based on star and half-star nanostructures. The pre-designed supramolecules could perfectly interact with CH3NH3PbI3 constituents and decelerate the degradation of crystalline perovskite areas during long-term air aging procedures by freezing morphology of active layers. PCE of perovskite + star P3HT/GQD and perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD devices only decreased from 18.27 to 9.57% as well as from 15.47 to 5.84%, respectively, within 150 days air aging. In this aging duration, the performance of unmodified CH3NH3PbI3 photovoltaics was plummeted from 10.77 to zero%. Star and half-star nano-hybrids also made a prominent progress in charge transportation even during long-term air agings.

ce:section.ce:para: Efficient establishment of solar cell technology on a global scale demands an improvement in the materials and devices to decrease the fabrication cost and increase the power conversion efficiency (PCE) [1,2]. Perovskite solar cells have recently attracted much attention due to their high performance, low-cost and low-temperature processes, and ease of fabrication [3–10]. Metal-organic halide perovskite solar cells possessed the PCE range of 3.8–22.7% [11–13], ascribing to their excellent electrical and optical properties [14–18]. In a strong contrast, the cell stability has become a severe hindrance toward the commercialization of perovskite photovoltaics [19–21]. The instability has been correlated with the organometallic absorber toward humidity [22] and their overall poor long-term thermal stability [23]. Several researches have been conducted to achieve better stability in the perovskite photovoltaics, e.g., two dimensional (2D) perovskite structure designing [24], cation engineering [25,26], an additive in the hole and electron transfer layer [27], a graphene layer on top of the perovskite/hole transporting thin film [28], etc. In recent years, the carbon-based nanomaterials like graphene [29,30], reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [31–33], and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [34–36] have been used to modify the light absorption layer to achieve a proper function in the perovskite solar cells.

Comparing to the traditional graphene materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new type of graphene nanostructures, which exhibit good crystallinity, quantum confinement, suitable chemical stability, well dispersing in various solvents [37–42], etc. Yang et al. [43] manifested that the GQDs could provide a superfast electron tunnel for the optoelectronic devices. The size effect and plasma treatment were investigated on the recombination resistance of the perovskite devices by incorporating the GQDs onto blocking TiO2 layer [44,45]. The electronic properties were also improved in the electron transport layer of SnO2 by the help of GQDs [46]. The GQDs were also employed for passivating the oxygen agents on the surface of ZnO [47], providing an effective charge-extraction pathway [48], engineering the multiple interfaces [49], etc. Shen et al. [50] used the GQD to modify the TiO2 electron transport layer of perovskite solar cells, yielding a maximum PCE of 20.45%.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are another class of carbon nanomaterials, which could be useful in the perovskite solar cells [51,52] by possessing the efficient electron transportation abilities, unique electron reservoir properties, and confinement effect [53–56]. Firstly, Paulo et al. [57] implemented the CQDs in the perovskite films as photovoltaic application. Li et al. [58] introduced for the first time a homogeneous CQDs/TiO2 nano-colloid to form the high quality electron transport layer for efficient perovskite solar cells. Wen et al. [59] then applied the CQDs as additive in the CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite films to reach a PCE of 19.17%. It is also witnessed that the CQDs could elevate the stability of perovskite solar cells [60–62]. A proper concentration of CQDs passivated the grain boundaries and decreased the trap-state density in the perovskite films to obtain an excellent environmental stability [63].

The star poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT)/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules were constructed and applied in the photoactive layers of CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite solar cells to stabilize the photovoltaic features. The PCE of perovskite + star P3HT/GQD and perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD devices only decreased from 18.27 to 9.57% as well as from 15.47 to 5.84%, respectively, within 150 days air aging. In this aging duration, the performance of unmodified CH3NH3PbI3 photovoltaics fell to zero% from 10.77%. In fact, the morphological modification of perovskite photoactive layers with the pre-developed nano-hybrids prevented them from degradation in air and humidity. The decoration of carbon-based quantum dots and their application in the photoactive layer of perovskite solar cells to simultaneously promote the efficiency and stability was the novelty of the current research. The released data were verified in a detailed procedure.

Preparation of CQDs. Citric acid and ethylenediamine were dissolved in deionized water, transferred to a desired autoclave and heated to 240 °C for 15 h and then cooled to the room temperature. The reaction solution was filtered to reach a dark filter solution, which was further dialyzed for 7 days and the CQDs were obtained by freeze-drying [64,65].

Preparation of GQDs. On the basis of the modified Hummers' method [66,67], the graphite and H2SO4 were stirred in an ice bath and KMnO4 was added dropwise. The solution was heated to 30 °C for 2 h and the deionizer water was added. The reaction was subsequently heated to 90 °C for 30 min and the deionizer water was added once again. When the temperature was cooled down to 60 °C, H2O2 was added to reach a yellow solution. The HCl solution was poured, the supernatant was decanted and centrifuged with deionizer water to pH = 4–6, and the mixture solution was dialyzed and the graphene oxide (GO) powder was obtained by freeze-drying. The GO precursors were then suspended in H2SO4 for 5 h and treated with KMnO4. The reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h and then heated to 70 °C for another 1 h. The solution was filtered with a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane and subsequently dialyzed and the GQD was collected [66].
Polymers synthesis. Regioregular P3HT (˃ 99%, number average molecular weights (Mn) = 8000 g/mol, dispersity = 1.20–1.25) was synthesized by Grignard metathesis polymerization [68].

Designing the QD/polymer nanostructures. The P3HT was dissolved in 1,2-dichlorobenzene (1,2-DCB) at 100 °C for 1 h and then added to the pre-dispersed QDs in the same solvent with a total concentration of 0.12 wt%. The resulted vials were subsequently introduced to the sonication step at 40 °C for 10 h and the crystallization step at 20 °C for 1 day.

Perovskite preparation. The perovskite solution was prepared by dissolution of lead (II) iodide (PbI 2) and methylamine iodide (MAI) (molar ratio of 1:1) in anhydrous N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) with a concentration of 38 wt%. The QD derivatives were added into the solution with a concentration of 0.10 mg mL−1 and the solution was stirred overnight in pure nitrogen atmosphere.

Photovoltaic cell fabrication. The perovskite solar cells were fabricated on fluorine-doped indium oxide (FTO) pattern glass substrates with a device configuration of FTO/b-TiO2/mpTiO2/CH3NH3PbI3/spiro-OMeTAD/MoO3/Ag. The FTO glass substrates were treated using the ultraviolet (UV)-ozone for 45 min. The 40 nm thick TiO2 was then spin coated from the butyl alcohol. The mesoporous TiO2 layer was spin coated from a solution having 18NRT Dyesol paste in ethanol within 40 s at 5000 rpm. The TiO2 coated substrates were cured for 30 min at 450 °C. The perovskite solution was spin coated on the mesoporous TiO2 layer at 5000 rpm. The perovskite films with the thicknesses of 400 nm were annealed at 150 °C for 20 min. The hole transfer material solution was prepared by mixing the spiro-OMeTAD, tert-butylpyridine, and bis(trifluoromethane) sulfonamide lithium salt (Li-TFSI) solution in chlorobenzene and spin coated onto the perovskite film at 3000 rpm for 1 min. Finally, a 100 nm thick Ag anode was deposited on the substrate via a shadow mask under 10−4 Pa. The measurements were done at room temperature and the currents were recorded under 100 mW cm−2 simulated AM 1.5G solar irradiation in the air. Solar cells were exposed to 100 mW/cm2 with AM 1.5G in 35% humidity in air at 30 °C and a continuous illumination was conducted. The devices were stored under the similar illumination and conditions between the J-V measurements.

The raw GQD and CQD nanoparticles were prepared to use as basic materials for the star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules. Firstly, the characteristics of QDs and their correlated nano-hybrids will be discovered and, secondly, they will be employed in the perovskite solar cells to efficiently stabilize them. The diameter range of CQD and GQD was 2–5 nm, as depicted in the transmission electron microscopic (TEM, Philips CM30) images of Fig. 1 (a) and (b), respectively. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of QDs in the inset panels of Fig. 1(a) and (b) demonstrated somewhat crystalline nature for them. The CQDs were quasi-spherical, whereas the GQDs were disc-like with the thickness of 0.5–4.0 nm.

The half-star P3HT/CQD and star P3HT/GQD supramolecules were constructed from the crystallization of P3HT chains onto the CQD and GQD nanoparticles, respectively. The GQDs with the flat configurations resulted in more mature nano-hybrids and thereby, the perfect star P3HT/GQD supramolecules were achieved. As illustrated in Fig. 1(c) and (d), the star P3HT/GQD nanostructures possessed more attached P3HT nanofibrils compared with the half-star P3HT/CQD counterparts. The SAED patterns in the inset panels of Fig. 1(c) and (d) reflected the (100) and (002) planes for a face-on orientation as well as the (020) and (002) planes for an edge-on orientation. The growth prisms in question were very sharp, which was a sign of high-quality crystallization in the P3HT chains.

The QDs and star/half-star P3HT/QD nanostructures were investigated by the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/Max-B diffractometer), photoluminescence (PL, optistatDry-BLV), and ultra-violet-visible (UV–Vis, Lambda 750) analyses. The XRD spectra were recorded for the pure CH3NH3PbI3 thin films and those modified through the GQD, star P3HT/GQD, CQD and half-star P3HT/CQD additives, as displayed in Fig. 2 (a). The arranged planes of (110), (112), (211), (220) and (312), belonged to the tetragonal CH3NH3PbI3, peaked at 14.89, 20.87, 24.11, 29.25 and 32.60°, respectively. The GQD sign was observed at 2θ peak of 23°, which was associated with a layer spacing around 3.85 Å. Likewise, the CQDs peaked at 2θ = 19° and possessed a d-spacing of 4.25 Å. These bared QDs decreased the crystal quality and, consequently, the intensity of identifying peaks. On the contrary, the P3HT/QD supramolecules, in particular the star P3HT/GQD nanostructures, enhanced the crystallinity and thus, the perovskite peaks intensities (Fig. 2(a)). Furthermore, the P3HT nanofibrils in the star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD nano-hybrids appeared at 2θ of 5.7°, originating from their well-ordered and crystalline structures. In addition to the upon-developed measurements, the XRD spectra were recorded within 150 days after sample preparation. During these 150 days, the thin films were exposed to air with 35% humidity at 30 °C. The XRD spectra of 150 day-aged samples are also illustrated in Fig. 2(a). Within the air aging, all XRD spectra were plummeted. However, this diminishing trend was different in various thin films. The full width half maximum (FWHM) data of star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD nano-hybrids were 0.011 and 0.013 Å−1, respectively. After 150 days aging, the respective data became 0.029 and 0.031 Å−1, respectively. The steepest decrement was detected for the pure CH3NH3PbI3 thin films. The GQD and CQD nanoparticles decreased the aforementioned trend and somehow retained the principal perovskite peaks. Hence, more intensified peaks appeared in the 150 day-aged samples of perovskite + GQD and perovskite + CQD than the primary CH3NH3PbI3 layers aged in similar conditions (Fig. 2(a)). Although the QDs were not capable of raising the crystallinity of perovskite films, they retained their ordering during air aging as far as possible. This role was much more conspicuous in the pre-designed star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules. The most intensified perovskite peaks were detected in the CH3NH3PbI3 + star P3HT/GQD systems after 150 days air aging. Similar results were acquired for the thin films based on the half-star P3HT/CQD nano-hybrids. The star/half-star P3HT/QD nanostructures not only promoted the perovskite crystallinity, but also perfectly retained this arrangement and ordering during the aging in air at 30 °C and humidity of 35%. Notably, the QD-based nanomaterials, particularly the pre-developed ones, strengthened the crystalline phase regions and stabilized the morphology.

The PL spectra of pure CH3NH3PbI3, perovskite + QD, and perovskite + star/half-star P3HT/QD systems in the upon-fabricated states and within 150 days air aging are displayed in Fig. 2(b). The primary CH3NH3PbI3 samples and those manipulated with the QDs represented a perovskite emission signal at around 769 nm. These peaks were significantly less intensified compared with those observed for the perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD and perovskite + star P3HT/GQD systems at about 773 nm (Fig. 2(b)). After 150 days air aging, the PL spectrum of pristine CH3NH3PbI3 thin films became to some extent flat. The pure GQDs and CQDs did not alter this flattening so much. In contrast, the star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules did it prominently and retained the intensity of PL spectra as far as possible. On the basis of the time resolved PL (TRPL) data obtained within 0, 50, and 150 days air aging, the star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules not only facilitated the charge transporting in the unaged samples, but also assisted it during the morphological degradation. The bared QDs also had better charge transporting results with respect to the pure layers during 50 and 150 days aging. The lifetime amounts of CH3NH3PbI3, perovskite + CQD, perovskite + GQD, perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD and perovskite + star P3HT/GQD systems were equal to (4.84, 3.67, 4.22, 19.58 and 19.87 ns), (2.20, 2.35, 2.69, 14.30 and 14.54 ns), and (0.12, 0.85, 0.93, 4.46 and 4.79 ns) within 0, 50, and 150 days air aging, respectively.

The consequences occurred in the UV–Vis measurements were completely in accordance with the aforementioned analyses. The UV–Vis spectra of the unmodified and modified thin films are exhibited in Fig. 2(c). The most intensified and red-shifted spectra belonged to the perovskite thin films composed of the pre-developed supramolecules, especially the star P3HT/GQD nanostructures. In the nano-hybrids in question, the crystallized P3HT chains peaked at 482 and 479 nm for the GQD- and CQD-based ones, respectively. According to UV–Vis spectra reported in Fig. 2(c), unlike the star and half-star additives, the bared QDs diminished the peak intensities and also blue-shifted them. By large, the perovskite peaks were in the wavenumber ranges of 440–475 and 710–850 nm. A stable morphology could be received through the grain boundary passivation and the proper interactions between the CH3NH3PbI3 and additives via carbon-carbon bonds. More stable morphologies could lead the higher log-term stability in the perovskite cells. Actually, the more integrated and interpenetrated morphology, the higher stability. All UV–Vis spectra were alleviated and somehow blue-shifted during the aging time. However, this decrement happened with a lower slope by the help of star a half-star supramolecules. The star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD nano-hybrids simultaneously increased the crystal quality (Fig. 2(a)), PL features (Fig. 2(b)), and absorbance (Fig. 2(c)) in both upon-fabricated and aged states.

The morphologies of distinct systems were also investigated via the field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM). The resulted images are displayed in Fig. 3 (a–e) and the average and maximum grain sizes within 0 and 150 days aging are also reported in Table 1 . The unmodified CH3NH3PbI3 thin films owned the average and maximum grain sizes of 196 and 348 nm, respectively (Fig. 3(a)). Although these dimensions were higher than those reached for the perovskite + CQD (79 and 207 nm) (Fig. 3(b)) and perovskite + GQD (83 and 214 nm) (Fig. 3(c)) thin films, the pure CH3NH3PbI3 did not retain the primary morphology as much as the QD-based samples. The perovskite crystalline grains were completely destroyed within 150 days exposure to the air with 35% humidity at 30 °C and the average and maximum sizes were decreased down to 12 and 18 nm, respectively. While the ultimate grains in the perovskite + CQD and perovskite + GQD systems averaged in 21 and 29 nm and maximized in 53 and 60 nm, respectively, after 150 days air aging. On the other side, the half-star P3HT/CQD and star P3HT/GQD supramolecules not only promoted the morphological parameters in the upon-constructed state, but also retained them during the air aging as far as feasible. The average grain sizes of upon-constructed CH3NH3PbI3 + half-star P3HT/CQD and CH3NH3PbI3 + star P3HT/GQD systems were equal to 594 and 612 nm, respectively. The correlated maximum sizes were 777 and 788 nm, respectively. The FESEM images of the thin films in question are shown in Fig. 3(d) and (e). Even after 150 days aging, the acceptable grain sizes were recorded for the systems modified with the pre-designed supramolecules. As tabulated in Table 1, the grain sizes of perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD and perovskite + star P3HT/GQD thin films within 150 days aging averaged in 415 and 523 nm and maximized in 572 and 653 nm, respectively. The morphologies of the mentioned samples after 150 days air aging are depicted in Fig. 4 (a) and (b). That is to say, more mature supramolecules, i.e., the star P3HT/GQD nanostructure, having more P3HT nanofibrils and flat GQD substrates acted better than the half-star P3HT/CQD nano-hybrids. It was perfectly evidenced by aforementioned analyses (XRD (Fig. 2(a)), PL (Fig. 2(b)), UV–Vis (Fig. 2(c)) as well as the morphological features (Fig. 3(d) and (e) and Fig. 4(a) and (b))). This appropriate function was also observed during the air aging at 30 °C and in 35% humidity.

In this section, the QDs and related supramolecules were utilized to stabilize the CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite solar cells. The schematic morphologies of CH3NH3PbI3 photoactive layers manipulated by the pure QDs and P3HT/QD nano-hybrids are illustrated in Fig. 5 in upon-prepared and aged states. Furthermore, the structure of a typical fabricated perovskite photovoltaic cell is represented in the left column of Fig. 5. As above discussed, the pre-developed nano-hybrids were able to modify and stabilize the CH3NH3PbI3 thin films. The stabilization of solar cells was studied under 35% air humidity at 30 °C. The proposed scheme in Fig. 5 is the assumption from the experimental results. We need further effort to verify the proposed mechanisms. In addition, the fundamental reasons and superiorities to apply star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/GQD to perovskite active layer to advance device performance are currently still ongoing in our laboratory.

The photovoltaic characteristics, including the short circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF), open circuit voltage (Voc), PCE, series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rtr), were gathered within 50, 100, and 150 days air aging and tabulated in Table 1. The current density-voltage (J-V) curves are also manifested in Fig. 6 for the upon-fabricated and 150 day-aged devices, respectively. The unmodified solar cells, composed of glass/FTO/TiO2/TiO2 + CH3NH3PbI3/CH3NH3PbI3/spiro-MeOTAD/Ag layers, reflected the Jsc, FF, Voc and PCE parameters of 17.82 mA/cm2, 65%, 0.93 V and 10.77%, respectively. The photovoltaic data of these pure cells without any additive significantly decreased during air aging at 30 °C in 35% humidity. All data details are reported in Table 1 for further clarification. The performances of CH3NH3PbI3 cells were plummeted to 4.18, 0.57, and 0.00% within 50, 100, and 150 days aging, respectively. Actually, the unmodified solar cells fabricated in this work lost their function in about 150 days exposure to the air. To prevent this steep degradation, the GQD, CQD, star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD nanostructures were incorporated into the CH3NH3PbI3 photoactive layers. Despite the fact that the GQD and CQD nanoparticles did not enhance the photovoltaic features in the upon-constructed state, they stabilized the cell parameters during the aging process. The pristine perovskite + GQD and perovskite + CQD systems owned the Jsc, FF, Voc and PCE of (17.41 mA/cm2, 64%, 0.93 V and 10.36%) and (16.89 mA/cm2, 63%, 0.92 V and 9.79%), respectively. The pure QDs were not capable of inducing the crystallinity and improving the morphology in the CH3NH3PbI3 layers, instead, they froze the perovskite precursors and decreased the rate of degradation during aging. Within 100 and 150 days exposure to the air, the perovskite + CQD cells represented the parameters of (10.32 and 5.73 mA/cm2), (43 and 31%), (0.53 and 0.30 V) and (2.35 and 0.53%), respectively. Similar but a bit better results were achieved for the perovskite + GQD systems, i.e., Jsc = 11.80 and 7.93 mA/cm2, FF = 46 and 35%, Voc = 0.57 and 0.36 V and, eventually, PCE = 3.09 and 1.00% within 100 and 150 days air aging, respectively. These outcomes were better than those observed in the unmodified CH3NH3PbI3 cells, which ended to a 0.00%.

Forwardly, the half-star P3HT/CQD and star P3HT/GQD supramolecules simultaneously promoted the efficacy and stability of perovskite solar cells. The upon-prepared and 150 day-aged J-V curves are represented in Fig. 6. As tabulated in Table 1, the best data were acquired for the perovskite + star P3HT/GQD devices, i.e., Jsc = 24.71 mA/cm2, FF = 77%, Voc = 0.96 V and PCE = 18.27%. The second best photovoltaic features were reached in the perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD systems (21.94 mA/cm2, 75%, 0.94 V and 15.47%). The star and half-star supramolecules could perfectly interact with the CH3NH3PbI3 constituents and freeze the morphology of active layers. The frozen morphologies were less degraded during the air aging. In previous sections, the XRD (Fig. 2(a)), PL (Fig. 2(b)), UV–Vis (Fig. 2(c)) and FESEM (Fig. 3(d) and (e) as well as Fig. 4(a) and (b)) analyses approved these claims. The mature star P3HT/GQD nano-hybrids played their role better than the half-star P3HT/CQD nanostructures. Because former ones possessed more compact and ordered P3HT arms, which were responsible for interacting with the perovskite precursors. Even the 50 day-aged perovskite + star P3HT/GQD solar cells acted better than the upon-fabricated perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD devices (15.90% versus 15.47%). This paramountcy was originated from a better Jsc (23.30 mA/cm2 versus 21.94 mA/cm2). The raised Voc may be originated from the increased effective band gap by comparison to the relative difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [69]. The morphological stabilization by the star P3HT/GQD nano-hybrids conspicuously contributed this phenomenon. This stabilization also continued during the long-term aging processes. The (Jsc, FF, Voc and PCE) were (21.54 mA/cm2, 71%, 0.84 V and 12.85%) and (19.05 mA/cm2, 67%, 0.75 V and 9.57%) within 100 and 150 days exposure to the air for the perovskite + star P3HT/GQD solar cells, respectively. During the same aging periods of time, similar but a bit lower results were also detected in the perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD devices (16.90 and 13.85 mA/cm2, 68 and 62%, 0.79 and 0.68 V and 9.08 and 5.84%). As expected, the star P3HT/GQD supramolecules not only led to the highest performance in the CH3NH3PbI3 solar cells, but also the most stabilized systems were reached during the long-term agings. The efficiencies of 18.27 and 9.57% appeared for the star-based photovoltaics in the upon-constructed and 150 day-aged states, respectively, which were the largest efficacies detected in the current work in similar conditions.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) spectra of different systems are reported in Fig. 7 . In this Figure, the filled and dashed curves stood for the unaged and aged (150 days) states, respectively. Nyquist curves were drawn in dark condition with Z′ (real part of impedance) versus Z″ (imaginary part of impedance) axes. The Rs and Rtr data, extracted from the model in inset of Fig. 7, are also reported in Table 1. The respective data are also graphically depicted in Fig. 8 . The semicircle radii or Rtr values of the pristine CH3NH3PbI3 devices and those manipulated through the CQD and GQD nanoparticles were 248.1, 263.4, and 255.7 Ω, respectively. A vigorous decrement of the Rtr was detected in the perovskite + star/half-star P3HT/QD systems, especially in the star P3HT/GQD based ones. The CH3NH3PbI3 thin films modified by the half-star P3HT/CQD and star P3HT/GQD supramolecules led to the Rtr's of 98.7 and 76.4 Ω, respectively. The resistance against the charge transferring increased during the aging period of time for all systems. It could be mainly assigned to the degradation of CH3NH3PbI3 morphology. Notably, the pre-designed supramolecules significantly slowed down the morphological degradation through constructing more stable crystalline phase regions in the CH3NH3PbI3 layers. Data details about the alterations in the charge resistances during air aging procedures (50, 100, and 150 days) are tabulated in Table 1. After 150 days exposure of the samples to the air at 30 °C in 35% humidity, the Rtr's were raised up to 308.4, 296.5, and 291.8 Ω for the CH3NH3PbI3, perovskite + CQD, and perovskite + GQD systems, respectively. The enhancing trend was slower in the QD based samples compared with the unmodified one, demonstrating that the CQD and GQD nanoparticles were somewhat able to resist against the morphological degradations. The incorporation of pure GQD and CQD partially stabilized the perovskite solar cells during the long-term agings. On the other side, the star and half-star nano-hybrids made a prominent progress in the charge transportation even during the long-term air agings. Within 50, 100, and 150 days aging in the perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD systems, the Rtr's of 192.5, 246.0, and 277.1 Ω were obtained, respectively. During the same aging periods, a bit better results were observed for the perovskite + star P3HT/GQD samples, i.e., 112.9, 201.6, and 224.7 Ω, respectively. More mature structure of star P3HT/GQD supramolecules, with respect to the half-star P3HT/CQD nano-hybrids, not only further promoted the morphological characteristics and thus the charge transporting, but also positively affected these features during the long-term air aging processes. The mentioned supramolecules decelerated the degradation of crystalline perovskite areas during the long-term air aging procedures. It could be attributed to the proper interactions between the additives and host CH3NH3PbI3 precursors.

The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra of CH3NH3PbI3 perovskites in the pure and mixed states with the QDs and their associated nano-hybrids within 0 and 150 days aging are displayed in Fig. 9 . The perovskite + star P3HT/GQD devices owned the most intensified EQE spectra. Towards the perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD, pure perovskite, perovskite + GQD and perovskite + CQD systems, the intensity was plummeted. This attenuation was much conspicuous in the pure perovskites and those manipulated with the bared QDs. The star and half-star supramolecules affected the EQE spectra even during the air aging at 30 °C and in 35% humidity. These nano-hybrids retained the intensity of EQE spectra as far as possible, as shown in Fig. 9 for the 150 day-aged solar cells (dashed curves). The largest perovskite grains, the highest degrees of crystallization, the lowest charge resistances and the greatest optical properties had the deep roots in the largest performances and the most stabilized devices. Although the QDs stabilized the perovskite photovoltaics and to some extent the EQE quality during aging, their impact was negligible with respect to the pre-developed star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules.

The star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD supramolecules were constructed and applied in the active layers of CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite solar cells to stabilize the photovoltaic features. Morphological modification of perovskite photoactive layers with the pre-developed nano-hybrids prevented them from degradation in air and humidity. Even after 150 days aging, the acceptable grain sizes were recorded for the CH3NH3PbI3 + half-star P3HT/CQD (415 nm versus 594 nm) and CH3NH3PbI3 + star P3HT/GQD (523 nm versus 612 nm) systems. The unmodified solar cells fabricated in this work lost their function in about 150 days exposure to the air. To prevent this steep degradation, the GQD, CQD, star P3HT/GQD and half-star P3HT/CQD nanostructures were incorporated into CH3NH3PbI3 photoactive layers. Despite the fact that the QDs did not enhance the photovoltaic features in the upon-constructed state, they partially stabilized the cell parameters during aging. Star and half-star supramolecules not only led to the highest performances in CH3NH3PbI3 solar cells (18.27 and 15.47%), but also the most stabilized systems were reached during the long-term agings (9.57 and 5.84%). The star and half-star nano-hybrids also promoted the charge transportation even during the long-term air agings. Within 150 days aging in the perovskite + star P3HT/GQD and perovskite + half-star P3HT/CQD systems, the Rtr's of 224.7 and 277.1 Ω were obtained, respectively. More mature structure of star P3HT/GQD supramolecules not only further promoted the morphological characteristics and thus the charge transporting, but also positively affected these features during the long-term air aging processes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

PMMA thin film with embedded carbon quantum dots for post-fabrication improvement of light harvesting in perovskite solar cells. Nanomaterials, 10(2), p.291.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots enhance the photocatalytic performance of BiVO 4 with different exposed facets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tang, D., Zhang, H., Huang, H., Liu, R., Han, Y., Liu, Y., Tong, C. and Kang, Z., 2013. Carbon quantum dots enhance the photocatalytic performance of BiVO 4 with different exposed facets. Dalton Transactions, 42(18), pp.6285-6289.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, Y., Yu, Y.X. and Zhang, W.D., 2013. Carbon quantum dots-doped CdS microspheres with enhanced photocatalytic performance. Journal of Alloys and Compounds, 569, pp.102-110.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering triangular carbon quantum dots with unprecedented narrow bandwidth emission for multicolored LEDs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuan, F., Yuan, T., Sui, L., Wang, Z., Xi, Z., Li, Y., Li, X., Fan, L., Tan, Z.A., Chen, A. and Jin, M., 2018. Engineering triangular carbon quantum dots with unprecedented narrow bandwidth emission for multicolored LEDs. Nature Communications, 9(1), pp.2249(1-11).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots as new hole transport material for perovskite solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Paulo, S., Stoica, G., Cambarau, W., Martinez-Ferrero, E. and Palomares, E., 2016. Carbon quantum dots as new hole transport material for perovskite solar cells. Synthetic Metals, 222, pp.17-22.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, H., Shi, W., Huang, W., Yao, E.P., Han, J., Chen, Z., Liu, S., Shen, Y., Wang, M. and Yang, Y., 2017. Carbon quantum dots/TiO x electron transport layer boosts efficiency of planar heterojunction perovskite solar cells to 19%. Nano letters, 17(4), pp.2328-2335.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving the power conversion efficiency of perovskite solar cells by adding carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wen, Y., Zhu, G. and Shao, Y., 2020. Improving the power conversion efficiency of perovskite solar cells by adding carbon quantum dots. Journal of Materials Science, 55(7), pp.2937-2946.

Passivation of the grain boundaries of CH3NH3PbI3 using carbon quantum dots for highly efficient perovskite solar cells with excellent environmental stability. Nanoscale, 11(1), pp.115-124.

Investigation of the enhanced performance and lifetime of organic solar cells using solution-processed carbon dots as the electron transport layers. Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 3(48), pp.12403-12409.

Hummers Jr, W.S. and Offeman, R.E., 1958. Preparation of graphitic oxide. Journal of the American Chemical Society, 80(6), pp.1339-1339.
